title: Autumn feeding: sugar syrup for winter stores
subtitle: Getting enough winter stores into the hive before the cold shuts down brood and movement.
excerpt: Colonies that go into winter without enough stores will starve by February even in a mild year. The window for autumn feeding in the UK is narrow: warm enough for bees to take down and ripen syrup, but late enough that they do not use fresh syrup to raise unnecessary brood. This covers the timing, the syrup concentration, and how much to feed.
sourceNotes:
National Bee Unit, Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024 edition), winter preparation section. https://www.nationalbeeunit.com
BBKA (British Beekeepers Association) winter preparation guidance.

content.text: Autumn feeding has one goal: get the colony into winter with enough
to survive until the first spring flows. In the UK that means having 15 to 20 kg of capped stores above the cluster before October turns cold enough to break the syrup-uptake chain.

In most parts of the UK the window falls between late August and mid-September. Earlier than that, and the colony is likely to use the syrup to raise another round of brood, which delays ripening. Later than mid-October, and the night temperatures drop below the range at which bees will take the syrup down and ripen it properly. Watch the colony rather than the calendar: feeding starts when the main flow is clearly over, brood is beginning to reduce, and the bees are bringing in little or no fresh nectar.

Use a heavy syrup of 2:1 sugar to water by weight (2 kg of white granulated sugar dissolved in 1 litre of warm water). Heavy syrup needs less water driven off by the bees to ripen to a storable moisture content, which means the colony does less work and uses less winter food in the process. Spring feeding uses a thinner 1:1 syrup to stimulate the colony; autumn feeding uses heavy.

Lift the hive gently at the back to gauge weight before feeding. A hive that feels light needs as much as 10 to 15 kg of stores. A 2:1 syrup converts to roughly half its volume in capped stores, so plan on feeding 8 to 10 litres of heavy syrup spread across several fills. Top up the
every two to three days until the bees stop taking it down readily, a sign the combs above are full.

Remove the feeder once the colony is no longer taking syrup. Leaving an empty feeder on over winter gives woodlice and wax moth somewhere to shelter, and adds a cold dead space above the cluster. Do one final heft before putting the hive to bed for winter to confirm the weight is right.
